title: Isaiah 54
subtitle: Sing, Barren Woman! The Covenant of Peace

heb.paragraph: "Sing, barren woman (ʿăqārâ), you who never bore a child; burst into song, shout for joy, you who were never in labour; because more are the children of the desolate woman than of her who has a husband" (v.1). Paul quotes this in Galatians 4:27 for the new covenant church. After the servant dies (ch.53), the barren woman sings (ch.54) — the servant death produces the barren woman children. "Enlarge the place of your tent, stretch your tent curtains wide" (v.2). "Your Maker is your husband — the LORD Almighty is his name" (v.5). "For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with deep compassion I will bring you back. In a surge of anger I hid my face from you for a moment, but with everlasting kindness I will have compassion on you" (vv.7–8). Brief anger versus everlasting kindness. "Though the mountains be shaken, my unfailing love for you will not be shaken, nor my covenant of peace be removed" (v.10).

notes.note: "Sing, barren woman!" Galatians 4:27 quotes this. The barren woman is the new covenant community — born not from human effort but from the servant death.
"Your Maker is your husband." The marriage metaphor: God as husband, Israel as wife. The marriage that 50:1 said was never divorced is now renewed.
"For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with deep compassion I will bring you back." Moment vs forever. Anger vs kindness. The ratio is overwhelmingly in favour of mercy.
"My unfailing love will not be shaken, nor my covenant of peace be removed." The covenant of peace: mountains shake, this does not. The most absolute security-promise in Isaiah.

refs.note: Paul quotes 'Sing, barren woman' to argue that the heavenly Jerusalem has more children than the earthly — allegory of Hagar and Sarah.
The children-too-many-for-your-space theme recurs: where did all these come from?
Enlarge the place of your tent echoes patriarchal blessing — room to spread out as YHWH prospers.
YHWH betrothing Israel in righteousness and love parallels the husband-of-your-youth imagery here.

hist.context: After the Fourth Servant Song, the mood shifts to exuberant celebration. The barren woman — Zion in exile — is called to sing because her children will outnumber those of the married woman (v.1). Enlarge your tent, stretch your curtains, lengthen your cords (vv.2–3). The husband-of-your-youth metaphor returns: YHWH abandoned Zion briefly in anger but now gathers her with everlasting compassion (vv.6–8). The flood-oath reference (v.9) grounds the promise: as YHWH swore never to flood the earth again, so he swears never to be angry with Zion again. The covenant is unbreakable.

sections.header: Verses 11–17 — “No Weapon Forged Against You Will Prevail”

heb.paragraph: "This is the heritage (naḥălâ) of the servants of the LORD, and this is their vindication from me" (v.17). The rebuilt city: sapphire foundations, ruby gates, sparkling jewels for walls (vv.11–12) — Revelation 21:18–21 develops this for the new Jerusalem. "All your children will be taught by the LORD, and great will be their peace" (v.13) — Jesus quotes this in John 6:45. "No weapon forged against you will prevail, and you will refute every tongue that accuses you" (v.17). The chapter that follows the servant death is entirely about security, beauty, and invincibility.

notes.note: "Afflicted city, I will rebuild you with precious stones. Your foundations with lapis lazuli, your battlements with rubies." Rev 21:18–21 builds the new Jerusalem from this blueprint.
"All your children will be taught by the LORD." John 6:45: Jesus quotes this for divine teaching that draws people to him. God as direct teacher — not through institutions alone but personally.
"No weapon forged against you will prevail." The definitive security promise. Not "no weapon will be forged" — weapons WILL come — but they will not succeed.

refs.note: The new Jerusalem's jeweled foundations — sapphire, emerald, ruby — draw on Isaiah's afflicted-city-rebuilt-with-gems vision.
Gold for bronze, silver for iron, peace as your governor — the later restoration oracle expands this gem-city imagery.
Jesus quotes 'they will all be taught by God' as fulfilled in those drawn to him by the Father.
They will all know me — the new-covenant promise parallels Isaiah's vision of divinely-instructed children.

hist.context: The afflicted, storm-tossed city will be rebuilt with precious stones: foundations of sapphires, battlements of rubies, gates of sparkling jewels (vv.11–12). All your children will be taught by YHWH, and great will be their peace (v.13). No weapon forged against you will prevail; every tongue that accuses you will be refuted (v.17). This is the heritage of YHWH's servants — plural, extending the Servant's vindication to those who belong to him. The chapter closes with one of the most sweeping security promises in Scripture: divine teaching, lasting peace, complete protection.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV“Enlarge the place of your tent, stretch your tent curtains wide, do not hold back; lengthen your cords, strengthen your stakes.ESV“Enlarge the place of your tent, and let the curtains of your habitations be stretched out; do not hold back; lengthen your cords and strengthen your stakes.NIVThe Lord will call you back as if you were a wife deserted and distressed in spirit— a wife who married young, only to be rejected,” says your God.ESVFor the LORD has called you like a wife deserted and grieved in spirit, like a wife of youth when she is cast off, says your God.

rec.text: ""Sing, barren woman!" Galatians 4:27 quotes this. The barren woman is the new covenant community — born not from human effort but from the servant death."

rows.label: No Weapon Prevails — 54:11–17
rows.text: Jewelled city (Rev 21:18). "All your children TAUGHT BY THE LORD" (John 6:45). "NO WEAPON forged against you will prevail."

hebtext.note: "Sing, barren woman (ʿăqārâ), you who never bore a child; burst into song, shout for joy, you who were never in labour; because more are the children of the desolate woman than of her who has a husband" (v.1). Paul quotes this in Galatians 4:27 for the new covenant church. After the servant dies (ch.53), the barren woman sings (ch.54) — the servant death produces the barren woman children. "Enlarge the place of your tent, stretch your tent curtains wide" (v.2). "Your Maker is your husband — the LORD Almighty is his name" (v.5). "For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with deep compassion I will bring you back. In a surge of anger I hid my face from you for a moment, but with everlasting kindness I will have compassion on you" (vv.7–8). Brief anger versus everlasting kindness. "Though the mountains be shaken, my unfailing love for you will not be shaken, nor my covenant of peace be removed" (v.10).

"This is the heritage (naḥălâ) of the servants of the LORD, and this is their vindication from me" (v.17). The rebuilt city: sapphire foundations, ruby gates, sparkling jewels for walls (vv.11–12) — Revelation 21:18–21 develops this for the new Jerusalem. "All your children will be taught by the LORD, and great will be their peace" (v.13) — Jesus quotes this in John 6:45. "No weapon forged against you will prevail, and you will refute every tongue that accuses you" (v.17). The chapter that follows the servant death is entirely about security, beauty, and invincibility.

themes.note: Covenant AND Mercy peak at 10: covenant of peace, barren woman sings, brief anger / everlasting kindness. The servant death (ch.53) produces the barren woman children (ch.54). No weapon prevails.

coaching.tip: 'Your Maker is your husband' (v. 5). After the Servant's suffering and death in chapter 53, chapter 54 explodes with restoration imagery — a barren woman sings, a rejected wife is reclaimed. The sequence matters: vicarious suffering (53) precedes joyful restoration (54). Isaiah's structure encodes the gospel pattern: cross before resurrection, death before new life, sacrifice before celebration.

'No weapon forged against you will prevail' (v. 17). This isn't a promise of comfort but of indestructibility — weapons will be forged, accusations will be launched, but none will succeed. Isaiah doesn't promise absence of attack but failure of attack. The promise is resilience, not immunity. The church under persecution has clung to this distinction for two millennia.
